records.ety: [F. datte, L. dactylus, fr. Gr. <?/, prob. not the same word as da`ktylos finger, but of Semitic origin.]

defs: The fruit of the date palm; also, the date palm itself.
notes: ☞ This fruit is somewhat in the shape of an olive, containing a soft pulp, sweet, esculent, and wholesome, and inclosing a hard kernel.

records.ety: [F. date, LL. data, fr. L. datus given, p. p. of dare to give; akin to Gr. <?/, OSlaw. dati, Skr. dā. Cf. Datum, Dose, Dato, Die.]

defs: That addition to a writing, inscription, coin, etc., which specifies the time (as day, month, and year) when the writing or inscription was given, or executed, or made; as, the date of a letter, of a will, of a deed, of a coin. etc.
quotes: And bonds without a date, they say, are void.

defs: The point of time at which a transaction or event takes place, or is appointed to take place; a given point of time; epoch; as, the date of a battle.
quotes: He at once, Down the long series of eventful time, So fixed the dates of being, so disposed To every living soul of every kind The field of motion, and the hour of rest.
What Time would spare, from Steel receives its date.

defs: Given or assigned length of life; duration.
quotes: Good luck prolonged hath thy date.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dated; p. pr. & vb. n. Dating.]

defs: To note the time of writing or executing; to express in an instrument the time of its execution; as, to date a letter, a bond, a deed, or a charter.
To note or fix the time of, as of an event; to give the date of; as, to date the building of the pyramids.
quotes: The letter is dated at Philadephia.
You will be suprised, I don't question, to find among your correspondencies in foreign parts, a letter dated from Blois.
In the countries of his jornal seems to have been written; parts of it are dated from them.
notes: ☞ We may say dated at or from a place.

defs: To have beginning; to begin; to be dated or reckoned; -- with from.
quotes: The Batavian republic dates from the successes of the French arms.
